title: The 3 Buckets I Use to Fund Early Retirement (Warts and All)
subtitle: A CD ladder, an equities engine, and a locked backstop — the real, imperfect portfolio structure behind my FIRE
description: The 3-bucket strategy I use to fund early retirement: a ~10-year CD ladder, an equities growth bucket, and a locked 401(k) backstop. Percentages, real trade-offs, and how it survived a 3.5% year.

faqs.question: What is the bucket strategy for retirement?
faqs.answer: It splits your portfolio into buckets by when you will spend the money: a cash or short-term bucket for near-term spending, a growth (equities) bucket for the medium term, and long-term or locked accounts for later. The point is to spend from cash during downturns instead of selling investments at a loss.

faqs.question: How many years of cash should the first bucket hold?
faqs.answer: There is no single right answer — anywhere from 2 to 10 or more years is common. I hold about 10 years of spending in a CD ladder because it lets me ignore market crashes entirely. A shorter cushion means less cash drag but requires more nerve.

faqs.question: Why use CDs instead of bonds for the cash bucket?
faqs.answer: For me, CDs offered a better rate than treasuries at the time, avoided the complexity of a bond portfolio for little extra yield, and their shorter maturities meant I was not locked out of higher rates later. A CD ladder also frees up predictable cash each year.

faqs.question: Does holding that much cash hurt your returns?
faqs.answer: Yes, on paper — cash earning around 4% underperforms equities at around 7% over time. I accept that drag as the price of never being forced to sell equities in a crash, and because the cash doubles as optionality: a potential home purchase, or dry powder to buy the dip.

faqs.question: What withdrawal rate does a bucket strategy support?
faqs.answer: My structure supports around 3%, but in practice it varies a lot year to year — I spent under 2% one year and closer to 3.5% another. Real spending is lumpy, so a rigid straight-line withdrawal rate is not how it actually works.
